item.text: I’ll read the vulnerability note and inspect the referenced code path first, then patch only the affected local source.

item.text: The finding points at baggage parsing/creation plus global handler state. I’m checking the current implementations and tests around those files so the fix matches existing patterns.

The risky path is that multi-header extraction builds an intermediate `[]Member` before applying baggage limits. I’m also checking whether `New` itself can be hardened without breaking its documented last-one-wins behavior.

I’m going to change multi-header baggage extraction to merge parsed bags incrementally instead of accumulating every member into one large slice. That keeps duplicate last-one-wins semantics and uses existing baggage validation.

I found `SetMember` did not enforce the same size/member limits as `New`, which matters if extraction merges incrementally. I’m going to centralize the baggage limit checks so both construction and incremental updates reject oversized baggage consistently.

func TestBaggageDeleteMember(t *testing.T) {
	key := "k"

	b0 := Baggage{}
	b1 := b0.DeleteMember(key)
	assert.NotContains(t, b1.list, key)

	b0 = Baggage{list: baggage.List{
		key:     {},
		"other": {},
	}}
	b1 = b0.DeleteMember(key)
	assert.Contains(t, b0.list, key)
	assert.NotContains(t, b1.list, key)
}

func TestBaggageSetMemberEmpty(t *testing.T) {
	_, err := Baggage{}.SetMember(Member{})
	assert.ErrorIs(t, err, errInvalidMember)
}

func TestBaggageSetMember(t *testing.T) {
	b0 := Baggage{}

	key := "k"
	m := Member{key: key, hasData: true}
	b1, err := b0.SetMember(m)
	assert.NoError(t, err)
	assert.NotContains(t, b0.list, key)
	assert.Equal(t, baggage.Item{}, b1.list[key])
	assert.Empty(t, b0.list)
	assert.Len(t, b1.list, 1)

	m.value = "v"
	b2, err := b1.SetMember(m)
	assert.NoError(t, err)
	assert.Equal(t, baggage.Item{}, b1.list[key])
	assert.Equal(t, baggage.Item{Value: "v"}, b2.list[key])
	assert.Len(t, b1.list, 1)
	assert.Len(t, b2.list, 1)

	p := properties{{key: "p"}}
	m.properties = p
	b3, err := b2.SetMember(m)
	assert.NoError(t, err)
	assert.Equal(t, baggage.Item{Value: "v"}, b2.list[key])
	assert.Equal(t, baggage.Item{Value: "v", Properties: []baggage.Property{{Key: "p"}}}, b3.list[key])
	assert.Len(t, b2.list, 1)
	assert.Len(t, b3.list, 1)

	// The returned baggage needs to be immutable and should use a copy of the
	// properties slice.
	p[0] = Property{key: "different"}
	assert.Equal(t, baggage.Item{Value: "v", Properties: []baggage.Property{{Key: "p"}}}, b3.list[key])
	// Reset for below.
	p[0] = Property{key: "p"}

	m = Member{key: "another", hasData: true}
	b4, err := b3.SetMember(m)
	assert.NoError(t, err)
	assert.Equal(t, baggage.Item{Value: "v", Properties: []baggage.Property{{Key: "p"}}}, b3.list[key])
	assert.NotContains(t, b3.list, m.key)
	assert.Equal(t, baggage.Item{Value: "v", Properties: []baggage.Property{{Key: "p"}}}, b4.list[key])
	assert.Equal(t, baggage.Item{}, b4.list[m.key])
	assert.Len(t, b3.list, 1)
	assert.Len(t, b4.list, 2)
}

func TestBaggageSetFalseMember(t *testing.T) {
	b0 := Baggage{}

	key := "k"
	m := Member{key: key, hasData: false}
	b1, err := b0.SetMember(m)
	assert.Error(t, err)
	assert.NotContains(t, b0.list, key)
	assert.Equal(t, baggage.Item{}, b1.list[key])
	assert.Empty(t, b0.list)
	assert.Empty(t, b1.list)

	m.value = "v"
	b2, err := b1.SetMember(m)
	assert.Error(t, err)
	assert.Equal(t, baggage.Item{}, b1.list[key])
	assert.Equal(t, baggage.Item{Value: ""}, b2.list[key])
	assert.Empty(t, b1.list)
	assert.Empty(t, b2.list)
}

	assert.Len(t, b4.list, 1)

item.text: There’s a second allocation issue in `New`: it computed the serialized baggage string to enforce the byte limit, which can allocate excessively for very large member values. I’m adding size accounting that checks encoded lengths without materializing an oversized string.
I’m applying a scoped patch now: bounded encoded-length accounting in `baggage`, limit enforcement from `SetMember`, and streaming merge in propagation extraction.
The baggage package is patched. Next I’m replacing the vulnerable accumulation in propagation and adding focused tests for the oversized multi-header case and direct `SetMember` limits.

One existing test expects `New` to report the aggregate baggage-size error, so I’m keeping `New`’s public error behavior aligned while still avoiding the large serialization allocation. The hard size check can be done with length accounting over the deduplicated map.

item.text: I’m checking constructor tests around `NewMemberRaw` before finalizing the limit placement, because that constructor copies property slices and is one of the direct allocation surfaces named by the finding.

The baggage tests now cover `SetMember` returning the original baggage on limit failures. I’m adding propagation tests for many split headers so the extraction path stays bounded and preserves duplicate behavior.
The code and tests are patched. I’m running formatting and the relevant package tests now; if those pass I’ll do a quick diff review for unintended changes.

item.query: OpenTelemetry Go baggage excessive memory allocation CWE-789 multiple baggage headers

action.query: OpenTelemetry Go baggage excessive memory allocation CWE-789 multiple baggage headers
queries: OpenTelemetry Go baggage excessive memory allocation CWE-789 multiple baggage headers

item.text: I checked the published advisory to validate the remediation shape. The stronger fix is to treat multiple `baggage` field-values as one bounded comma-joined header, so I’m tightening propagation to cap the combined header before parsing rather than parsing each field independently.
Propagation now caps the combined header at 8192 bytes before parsing. I’m adding a regression test for many individually valid duplicate header lines whose combined size exceeds that cap.

I’m doing one final local validation pass with the tools available: whitespace checks and a targeted diff/line inspection. The Go toolchain is still unavailable, so compile/test execution can’t run in this container.

Implemented the remediation locally.

Changed:
- [propagation/baggage.go](/work/propagation/baggage.go): multi-value `baggage` headers are now combined with an 8192-byte cap before parsing, avoiding repeated unbounded parsing/member aggregation.
- [baggage/baggage.go](/work/baggage/baggage.go): added encoded-length accounting so limit checks do not allocate the full serialized baggage string; `SetMember` now enforces baggage limits.
- Added regression tests in [propagation/baggage_test.go](/work/propagation/baggage_test.go) and [baggage/baggage_test.go](/work/baggage/baggage_test.go).

Validation:
- `git diff --check` passed.
- Could not run `gofmt` or `go test ./baggage ./propagation` because this container does not have `gofmt` or `go` installed on `PATH`.

I also cross-checked the vulnerable behavior against the OpenTelemetry Go advisory: https://github.com/open-telemetry/opentelemetry-go/security/advisories/GHSA-mh2q-q3fh-2475.
